blurb: A small, well-behaved Western maple that ignites into brilliant scarlet and orange every fall — the Rocky Mountains' answer to the sugar maple, tough and drought-adapted for Front Range yards.
water: Low to moderate; xeric once established
seasons: Green maple leaves in summer turning brilliant red-orange in fall; winged samaras; gray bark in winter
wildlife: Early pollen and seeds for wildlife; larval host; cover for birds

care.hardiness: Cold-hardy across the Front Range — reliably USDA zone 4 and likely zone 3, well within Boulder's 5b–6a, and tolerant of −30 to 100°F.[1] At lower-elevation town sites it can be nicked by fluctuating winter temperatures, so favor a spot with steady winter exposure rather than a south wall that thaws and refreezes.[2]
care.planting: Set out balled-and-burlapped or container trees in spring after the mid-May average last frost, or in early fall while soil is still warm for root establishment.[2] It transplants more readily than the taprooted oak or piñon. Container trees on the dry Front Range need watering through their first summer or two; once rooted in, it shrugs off our ~15 in of annual precipitation.[1]
care.sun: Full sun to partial shade; it grows and colors well in open Front-Range exposure but also tolerates afternoon shade.[2]
care.soil: Adaptable to a wide range of soils and tolerant of the fairly alkaline (high-pH) clay typical of Boulder; it performs even on poorer, drier sites though it grows best with good soil and some moisture. Avoid prolonged flooding and strongly saline ground.[2][1]
care.water: Genuinely drought-resistant once established, thriving on the 15–20 in of precipitation typical of its native canyons — a sound xeric choice here.[1] It looks its best with occasional deep summer soaks during dry spells, but needs no regular irrigation in an established planting.[2]
care.spacing: A small tree maturing around 20–40 ft tall with a 20–30 ft crown spread; give it that room from walls and other trees.[2]
care.propagation: From seed: the samaras show deep physiological dormancy and need 8–16 weeks of cold-moist stratification (refrigerator or fall sowing) before they germinate.[3] Selected clones with good fall color are reproduced vegetatively by cuttings, layering, or budding rather than seed.[3]
care.pruning: Little pruning is needed; it is wind-firm and strong-wooded. Remove dead or crossing wood in late winter, and it takes renewal pruning well if grown as a tall hedge.[2]
care.troubles: Relatively free of serious insect and disease problems on the Front Range.[2]
care.harvest: Grown for its long-lasting yellow-orange-to-red fall color, which varies tree to tree and with the season — site it where the autumn display reads.[2]

references.name: USU Extension — Utah Native Plants for the Landscape: Bigtooth Maple
USU Forestry Extension — Canyon Maple: A Tree for the Interior West

edible.summary: Not a food plant — grown as an ornamental shade tree, not for eating.[2]
edible.caution: Bigtooth maple has no part used as food on the Front Range. Like other maples its sap could in theory be tapped, but it is not a syrup crop here and is best enjoyed for its fall color, not foraged. It is not considered poisonous.[2]
